Name: joint_rate_distortion_compression
Title: Joint Rate-Distortion Optimization for Quantization and Pruning
Short Hypothesis: Quantization and pruning can be jointly optimized under a unified rate-distortion framework, leading to tighter operational bounds and more efficient model compression.
Related Work: Prior work has independently explored quantization (e.g., AMP-ViT) and pruning (e.g., Successive Pruning for Model Compression via Rate Distortion Theory) using rate-distortion principles. However, these methods treat quantization and pruning as separate processes, missing potential synergies. Recent advancements in mixed-precision quantization (e.g., EMQ) and adaptive pruning frameworks (e.g., HiLoRA) highlight the need for a unified approach. This proposal distinguishes itself by jointly optimizing quantization and pruning under a single rate-distortion objective, providing theoretical guarantees and practical algorithms.
Abstract: Model compression techniques such as quantization and pruning are essential for deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained devices. While these methods have been studied independently, their joint optimization remains unexplored. This paper proposes a unified rate-distortion framework for jointly optimizing quantization and pruning. By framing both techniques as lossy source coding problems, we derive operational bounds on the bits-per-weight required to preserve predictive sufficiency. Our approach leverages mutual information estimators to allocate bits where they reduce task-relevant distortion most. We introduce a practical algorithm that alternates between quantization and pruning steps, guided by a rate-distortion tradeoff. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet demonstrate that our method achieves superior compression ratios while maintaining model accuracy, outperforming state-of-the-art independent quantization and pruning techniques. Our theoretical analysis provides insights into the fundamental limits of joint compression, bridging the gap between information theory and practical model compression.
Experiments: Derive theoretical bounds on the joint rate-distortion tradeoff for quantization and pruning using mutual information estimators.
Develop an alternating optimization algorithm that jointly quantizes and prunes a neural network, guided by the derived bounds.

Evaluate the algorithm on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets using ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 architectures. Compare against state-of-the-art independent quantization (e.g., EMQ) and pruning (e.g., HiLoRA) methods.
Measure compression efficiency (bits-per-weight), model accuracy, and computational overhead. Use distortion-rate curves to visualize the tradeoff between compression and accuracy.
Analyze the sensitivity of the method to hyperparameters such as the distortion metric and the mutual information estimator.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The joint optimization may introduce additional computational overhead compared to independent methods.
The theoretical bounds rely on assumptions about the source distribution, which may not hold in practice.
The method’s performance may degrade for architectures with highly irregular weight distributions.
The mutual information estimators used may suffer from bias or variance, affecting the optimization process.
